progressive__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive (gpt-5.5)
progressive__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper proposes an intuitively simple idea—reordering/repeating training samples for LSTM-based video recognition inspired by spaced repetition—but in its current form the work appears substantially underdeveloped for a top-tier conference. The motivation is speculative and anthropomorphic, relying heavily on loose analogies to human memory without a rigorous connection to optimization, sequence modeling, curriculum learning, data augmentation, or sampling theory. The technical contribution is not yet clearly defined: the “stepped sampler” is described only vaguely in the abstract/introduction, and it is unclear how it differs from standard batching, repeated sampling, truncated sequence overlap, curriculum schedules, or epoch-level reshuffling. The related work is broad but not well targeted; it omits highly relevant areas such as curriculum learning, experience replay, temporal sampling for video models, sequence batching strategies, and repeated/oversampling effects in SGD. The experimental claims also seem weakly supported from the presented text: faster training-loss convergence and smoother curves are not sufficient evidence of improved generalization, and the paper needs stronger baselines, multiple seeds, statistical analysis, ablations over repetition/step parameters, compute-matched comparisons, and evaluation against modern video-recognition approaches rather than only a PyTorch LSTM sampler baseline. Clarity is a major issue throughout: the writing contains frequent grammatical errors, imprecise terminology, unsupported causal claims, and confusing statements such as interpreting test accuracy as “temporal information.” Overall, the paper may contain a potentially useful empirical observation about sample-ordering effects, but it needs a much clearer algorithmic specification, a more grounded theoretical or optimization-based rationale, substantially stronger experiments, and significant rewriting before its contribution can be properly assessed.

comments.title: Abstract overstates the stepped sampler's test-accuracy advantage
comments.quote: Meanwhile, it can maintain a higher test accuracy, compared with the traditional sampler.
comments.explanation: The abstract presents the stepped sampler as generally maintaining higher test accuracy than the traditional sampler, but the later results are mixed. For example, in the batch-size-25 setting, the traditional sampler reportedly reaches 0.656 test accuracy while the best stepped-sampler setting shown there reaches 0.603, and the authors themselves note that the traditional batch sampler has slightly higher test accuracy in that case. The stepped sampler appears to outperform in some settings, especially batch size 15, but the abstract should qualify the claim by saying that accuracy improvements are parameter-dependent rather than general.

comments.title: Human-learning and biological-network conclusions overreach beyond the sampler experiment
comments.quote: The results show that, there may be a close relationship between biological neural network and artificial neural network, whatever in structure and even in principle.
comments.explanation: Several passages infer human-like learning, similarity between human and machine learning, or a close biological-artificial neural-network relationship from the stepped-sampler results. The Ebbinghaus analogy may be useful as motivation, but the experiment only compares data-feeding schedules for a CNN-LSTM on UCF101. It does not measure human learning, biological neural networks, memory mechanisms, or structural/principled equivalence between biological and artificial systems. Moreover, the implemented sampler appears to use immediate adjacent overlap rather than psychological spaced repetition in the usual sense. The observed effects could be due to ordinary machine-learning factors such as repeated sample exposure, altered gradient variance, changed optimizer-update counts, order effects, or implicit curriculum effects. These claims should therefore be framed as motivation or speculation, not as conclusions verified by the experiments.

comments.title: Temporal information is conflated with accuracy and sampler-induced redundancy
comments.quote: We try to apply the mathematical equations to our experimental results, and analyze that the test accuracy in the experiment is the temporal information between video frames.
comments.explanation: The paper repeatedly treats temporal information too loosely. Test accuracy is a performance metric computed from predictions and labels, whereas temporal information is a property of the input sequence, such as frame-to-frame dependence or motion. Similarly, overlapping or repeating samples can increase redundancy or autocorrelation in the training order, but it does not necessarily increase the intrinsic temporal information between video frames. At most, accuracy or loss could be indirect outcomes affected by how well the model uses temporal information. The paper needs a clearer distinction between intrinsic temporal structure in the data, correlations induced by the sampler, and downstream performance metrics.

comments.title: Gibbs sampling is inaccurately characterized as sequence-data sampling
comments.quote: Gibbs sampling randomly selects data from an initial input sequence, and iterates according to the specified conditional probabilities, which are related to the required probability distribution of the final sampling data.
comments.explanation: This description does not match the standard definition of Gibbs sampling. Gibbs sampling is an MCMC method that samples components of a random vector from their conditional distributions given the other components, targeting a joint distribution. It is not normally a method for randomly selecting data from an initial input sequence. Since the paper's proposed sampler is a deterministic or structured minibatch-loading procedure, the analogy to Gibbs sampling may confuse readers about what kind of sampling problem is being addressed.

comments.title: Stepped-sampler comparison is confounded by effective batch size, sample exposure, optimizer updates, and computation
comments.quote: The difference of the models is only the sampler, for comparing the results only caused by the sampler.
comments.explanation: The stepped sampler appears to do more than reorder the same examples. A batch of size L is divided into overlapping sub-batches of size m with stride n, so examples may be presented multiple times, the model may receive sub-batches smaller than the nominal batch, and the number of forward/backward passes or optimizer updates per epoch may differ from the traditional BatchSampler baseline. The paper also does not specify whether losses are averaged or summed across sub-batches, whether optimizer steps occur after each sub-batch or original batch, or how LSTM hidden states are reset, detached, or carried. As a result, faster loss decrease per epoch could reflect more gradient updates, more effective sample presentations, a different mini-batch size, or more computation rather than the proposed repetition principle. Comparisons should be normalized or reported by optimizer updates, unique examples, sample presentations, and wall-clock/compute budget.

comments.title: Stepped-sampler algorithm and boundary behavior are ambiguous as written
comments.quote: if len(step_batch[]) == m then ... return step batch[]; ... idx = idx − m + n
comments.explanation: The intended sliding-window procedure is not reproducible from the pseudocode. It uses a for-loop over idx while also manually modifying idx, which can produce different behavior depending on programming-language conventions. It also says `return step batch[]`; in ordinary pseudocode or Python, `return` exits the procedure, so the subsequent reset and index update would not execute unless the authors actually mean `yield` or 'output and continue.' Boundary behavior is also underspecified when L − m is not divisible by n: the implementation might drop tail samples, emit shorter final sub-batches, pad, wrap, or force the last window to end at L. This matters for reported settings such as L=25, m=20, n=2 or 3, where some samples may receive fewer or no updates under a simple floor rule. The paper should explicitly define the iteration rule, output semantics, and treatment of leftover samples.

comments.title: Rationale for using an old PyTorch version is not a valid experimental control
comments.quote: The reason why we chose old version PyTorch is, the performance of the old version may not be so powelful, however, the experimental effect may be better, since the contrast of the results may be larger.
comments.explanation: Fixing a software version is useful for reproducibility, and using the same environment across methods can control implementation variation. However, choosing an older framework because it may produce larger contrasts is not a standard scientific control. The number of available PyTorch functions also does not reduce interference unless those functions affect the executed experiment. Older kernels, defaults, and performance characteristics may introduce their own confounders. The paper can report the version used, but this rationale does not justify the choice experimentally.

comments.title: Adam optimizer configuration is underspecified
comments.quote: We used Adam as the optimization algorithm. ... The learning rate was set to 0.0001. The momentum was set to 0.01.
comments.explanation: The optimizer and learning rate are stated, but the reported 'momentum' parameter is ambiguous because standard PyTorch Adam is parameterized by betas, epsilon, weight decay, and related options rather than a single SGD-style momentum value. The authors may mean beta1=0.01 or may be describing the configuration informally, but the exact Adam settings are not recoverable. Since convergence speed and stability are central outcomes in the paper, the optimizer hyperparameters should be specified in PyTorch terms.

comments.title: UCF101 train/test split procedure is underspecified
comments.quote: UCF101 was split into training set and test set by a ratio of 3:1.
comments.explanation: UCF101 has commonly used official train/test splits, and action-recognition results can vary depending on which videos, subjects, and classes are assigned to each split. The paper does not state whether the 3:1 split is random, stratified by class, based on official split files, fixed by a random seed, or repeated over multiple splits. Because the reported accuracy differences across sampler variants can be relatively close, the exact split protocol is important for reproducibility and for assessing whether observed differences are due to the sampler or split variance.

comments.title: Data preprocessing and augmentation are too vague to reproduce
comments.quote: The data transformation was applied to enhance the network. The input frames were transformed into 256 × 342 pixels.
comments.explanation: The resize target is stated, but 'data transformation' is too broad for reproducibility. In video recognition, frame sampling rate, clip length, crop strategy, horizontal flipping, normalization statistics, color jitter, and whether transformations are applied consistently across frames can materially affect accuracy and temporal structure. Since the paper attributes performance differences to the sampler, the preprocessing and augmentation pipeline should be specified well enough to rule out hidden confounders.

comments.title: Convergence, stability, and accuracy claims lack quantitative criteria and statistical support
comments.quote: The experimental results show that, compared with the traditional sampler, the training loss of the stepped sampler converges faster, and is more stable after the convergence, i.e., there is no large jitter after convergence.
comments.explanation: The paper relies heavily on visual comparisons of loss curves and single reported accuracy values, but it does not define a convergence criterion, stability metric, jitter measure, or statistical test. It also does not report multiple seeds, standard deviations, confidence intervals, or run-to-run variability. Therefore claims such as 'converges faster,' 'more stable,' and 'significantly higher' are not adequately supported. The figures may justify exploratory observations for particular runs and settings, but stronger conclusions require quantified metrics and uncertainty estimates.

comments.title: Stride-distribution and broad-spectrum-effect claims overinterpret limited experiments
comments.quote: If the batch size and the step size are fixed, the detection effect seems to be a normal distribution of the step stride.
comments.explanation: The reported experiments may suggest that an intermediate stride works better than very small or very large strides in some settings, but describing the effect as a normal distribution implies a specific parametric shape that is not established by a small sweep. Similarly, faster convergence in one CNN-LSTM architecture on one dataset with a few batch/stride settings does not establish a broad-spectrum effect across machine learning. These claims should be limited to the tested configurations unless supported by broader experiments across tasks, architectures, datasets, and seeds.

comments.title: Test-time shuffling is ambiguous and the ShuffleNet justification is inapplicable
comments.quote: Our test uses the shuffle operation. ShuffleNet (Zhang et al., 2018) proves that the shuffle operation can improve the image detection mAP.
comments.explanation: The paper does not clearly state what is shuffled during testing: the order of independent videos/clips, the order of frames within a video, or something else. These choices have very different implications. Shuffling independent test samples is usually harmless if model state is reset appropriately, but shuffling frames within videos would destroy temporal structure in an action-recognition task. The appeal to ShuffleNet does not justify the procedure, because ShuffleNet's shuffle is channel shuffle inside a CNN architecture, not dataset-order or frame-order shuffling. The paper should define the test-time shuffle operation and explain why it preserves the intended evaluation.

progressive_original__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive Original (gpt-5.5)
progressive_original__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper proposes an intuitively simple idea—reordering/repeating training samples for LSTM-based video recognition inspired by spaced repetition—but in its current form the work appears substantially underdeveloped for a top-tier conference. The motivation is speculative and anthropomorphic, relying heavily on loose analogies to human memory without a rigorous connection to optimization, sequence modeling, curriculum learning, data augmentation, or sampling theory. The technical contribution is not yet clearly defined: the “stepped sampler” is described only vaguely in the abstract/introduction, and it is unclear how it differs from standard batching, repeated sampling, truncated sequence overlap, curriculum schedules, or epoch-level reshuffling. The related work is broad but not well targeted; it omits highly relevant areas such as curriculum learning, experience replay, temporal sampling for video models, sequence batching strategies, and repeated/oversampling effects in SGD. The experimental claims also seem weakly supported from the presented text: faster training-loss convergence and smoother curves are not sufficient evidence of improved generalization, and the paper needs stronger baselines, multiple seeds, statistical analysis, ablations over repetition/step parameters, compute-matched comparisons, and evaluation against modern video-recognition approaches rather than only a PyTorch LSTM sampler baseline. Clarity is a major issue throughout: the writing contains frequent grammatical errors, imprecise terminology, unsupported causal claims, and confusing statements such as interpreting test accuracy as “temporal information.” Overall, the paper may contain a potentially useful empirical observation about sample-ordering effects, but it needs a much clearer algorithmic specification, a more grounded theoretical or optimization-based rationale, substantially stronger experiments, and significant rewriting before its contribution can be properly assessed.

comments.title: Abstract overstates test-accuracy advantage of the stepped sampler
comments.quote: Meanwhile, it can maintain a higher test accuracy, compared with the traditional sampler.
comments.explanation: At first this reads as a broad empirical claim: the proposed sampler not only stabilizes/converges faster, but also achieves higher test accuracy than traditional PyTorch sampling. I checked the later experimental context to see whether this is consistently supported. It is not: for batch size 25, the paper reports that the traditional sampler reaches 0.656 test accuracy, while the best stepped-sampler setting shown there reaches 0.603, and the authors explicitly state that the traditional batch sampler has a slightly higher test accuracy in that case. The stepped sampler is reported to outperform for at least one batch-size setting, especially batch size 15, but the abstract’s unqualified wording is too strong. A more accurate claim would distinguish convergence/stability from accuracy and state that accuracy is sometimes higher depending on batch/stride settings.

comments.title: Test accuracy is conflated with temporal information
comments.quote: We try to apply the mathematical equations to our experimental results, and analyze that the test accuracy in the experiment is the temporal information between video frames.
comments.explanation: I initially interpreted this as informal shorthand: perhaps the authors mean that improved test accuracy is evidence that the model has exploited temporal information better. Looking at the surrounding context, however, the wording remains stronger than that. Test accuracy is a performance metric computed from predicted and true labels; temporal information is a property of the input sequence, such as frame-to-frame dependence, motion, or correlation. Even if Appendix A defines temporal information via correlations, accuracy cannot itself be identified with that temporal information. At most, accuracy could be an indirect outcome influenced by how well temporal information is modeled. As written, the claim risks a category error and would need either a precise formal definition linking the two or a much weaker formulation.

comments.title: Human-like characteristics are inferred too strongly from sampler performance
comments.quote: Moreover, we believe that artificial neural networks have human-like characteristics from the experimental performance.
comments.explanation: The motivation from Ebbinghaus and repeated review is understandable, and the proposed overlapping/repeated batching scheme may indeed affect optimization. I checked whether the surrounding sections provide evidence that the effect specifically arises from a human-memory-like mechanism rather than from more ordinary machine-learning effects such as repeated exposure, altered effective sample weighting, changed gradient variance, curriculum-like ordering, or temporal overlap between adjacent clips. The context does not appear to isolate these alternatives. Therefore the experimental performance may support the practical utility of this sampler under some settings, but it does not justify the broader conclusion that artificial neural networks have human-like characteristics. This is a conceptual overclaim rather than a notation or OCR issue.

comments.title: RNN recurrence is described ambiguously as feeding previous output as next input
comments.quote: RNN takes the output of the previous time period as the input of the next time period, effectively using the temporal information of the input sequence.
comments.explanation: This may be intended as a simplified introduction, and if “output” means the recurrent hidden state emitted by the previous cell, the idea is close to standard RNN intuition. However, in conventional notation an RNN computes h_t from x_t and h_{t-1}, and then optionally computes an external output y_t from h_t. The previous external output y_{t-1} is not generally the input at the next step except in autoregressive sequence models. Because the passage does not distinguish hidden state from model output, a careful reader could misunderstand the architecture being described. This is minor in an introductory paragraph, but the statement should be tightened to say that the hidden state from the previous time step is passed forward.

comments.title: LSTM is said to avoid vanishing/exploding gradients too categorically
comments.quote: LSTM adds gates to RNN, thus it can effectively avoid the problem of gradient disappearance or explosion.
comments.explanation: The authors are right that LSTMs were introduced partly to address the vanishing-gradient problem in standard RNNs, and their gated memory cell helps preserve gradients over longer time spans. However, the standard result is mitigation, not guaranteed avoidance. LSTMs can still suffer from optimization issues, and exploding gradients can still occur in recurrent models, which is why gradient clipping remains common. The sentence is acceptable as broad motivation, but technically it overstates what LSTM gates guarantee.

comments.title: Gibbs sampling is characterized as a data-sequence sampler rather than an MCMC method over variables
comments.quote: Gibbs sampling randomly selects data from an initial input sequence, and iterates according to the specified conditional probabilities, which are related to the required probability distribution of the final sampling data.
comments.explanation: I tried to read this charitably as a high-level description of an iterative sampler, but it does not match the standard definition very well. Gibbs sampling is an MCMC method that samples components of a random vector from their conditional distributions given the other components, targeting a joint distribution. It is not normally described as randomly selecting data from an initial input sequence. This matters because the paper’s proposed sampler is a deterministic/structured data-loading procedure for minibatches, whereas Gibbs sampling is a probabilistic inference algorithm; the analogy may therefore confuse readers about what kind of sampling problem is being addressed. Since this is in related work rather than the core method, the issue is localized.

comments.title: Experimental speedup is overstated as evidence of human-like learning
comments.quote: We believe that the experimental results in Section 4 could prove that there is a certain correlation between human learning and machine learning, since the machine learning method with timely review and spaced repeat has a faster learning effect, compared with the machine learning without the human-like method.
comments.explanation: I can see the authors' intended argument: the sampler is inspired by Ebbinghaus-style review, and if it improves LSTM training, that supports the usefulness of the analogy. However, even if Section 4 shows faster convergence for the stepped sampler, that would not by itself prove a correlation between human learning and machine learning. Many purely optimization-related mechanisms could explain the result, such as repeated gradient updates on overlapping samples, changed effective epoch size, altered sample ordering, or implicit curriculum effects. The claim would be safer as a motivation or analogy, not as something proved by the experiments.

comments.title: Implementation is described as spaced review, but the sampler appears to use adjacent overlap
comments.quote: since the machine learning method with timely review and spaced repeat has a faster learning effect, compared with the machine learning without the human-like method.
comments.explanation: The Ebbinghaus motivation is understandable, and Figure 1 discusses spaced repetition over days. But the later sampler description says that the back of one sub-batch is the same as the front of the next sub-batch, i.e. immediate adjacent repetition within a batch. That is not the same intervention as spaced repetition or timely review in the usual psychological sense. The context partly resolves this by saying the authors also use an adjacent-repetition principle, but the prose still risks implying that the experimental method directly implements spaced review. A careful reader could therefore overinterpret the psychological analogy.

comments.title: Claim that repetition enhances temporal information is not technically justified
comments.quote: We believe that this part repetition enhances the correlation of the input frames, thereby enhancing the temporal information of the input frames, according to our definition of the temporal information in Appendix A.
comments.explanation: The authors consistently frame temporal information as correlation, so I understand why overlapping sub-batches are said to increase correlation in the presented input stream. What remains problematic is the jump from sampler-induced duplication to enhanced temporal information of the video frames. Repeating the same samples can increase redundancy or autocorrelation in the training order, but it does not necessarily increase the intrinsic temporal information between frames, nor does it show that the LSTM is learning better temporal dependencies rather than receiving more repeated gradient signal from some samples. This is especially important because the main contribution is attributed to better temporal fusion.

comments.title: Ambiguity between overlapping sub-batches and overlapping batches
comments.quote: The structure of the stepped sampler shows that, the input data of different batches is partly duplicated.
comments.explanation: Earlier in the same section, the sampler is described as dividing a batch into sub-batches, with overlap between adjacent sub-batches. Under that reading, the method is internally coherent. But this sentence says that different batches are partly duplicated, which would be a materially different sampling procedure: overlap across PyTorch batches would change epoch composition and sample frequency differently from overlap only within a batch. This may simply be imprecise wording, but because the method is a sampler and reproducibility depends on exactly where duplication occurs, the ambiguity could mislead implementation.

comments.title: Training procedure for overlapping sub-batches is underspecified
comments.quote: Finally, the data in each batch are divided into sub-batches with the same strides by the stepped sampler.
comments.explanation: The high-level idea of forming overlapping sub-batches is clear, and Figure 2 helps convey the intended indexing. However, the passage does not specify several choices that materially affect an LSTM experiment: whether each sub-batch produces a separate forward/backward pass, whether the optimizer steps after every sub-batch or after the original batch, whether losses are averaged or summed across overlapping sub-batches, and whether LSTM hidden states are reset, detached, or carried between sub-batches. These choices change both the effective number of gradient updates per epoch and the temporal signal seen by the model. Without them, a reader cannot reliably reproduce the method or assess whether the comparison to the ordinary batch sampler is controlled.

comments.title: Rounding the iteration count may silently drop samples and change the comparison
comments.quote: If _d_ is not an integer, PyTorch will round down to ensure that _d_ is an integer.
comments.explanation: Assuming the intended iteration count is the standard sliding-window count, roughly \(d = (L-m)/n + 1\), rounding down is a plausible implementation choice. But the consequence is not discussed. For settings such as batch size 25, step size 20, and stride 2, the final part of the original batch would not be covered by any full sub-batch under a simple floor rule. That means some samples may receive fewer or no updates within an epoch, contradicting the surrounding language that the batch sampler traverses all data. If instead the implementation pads, emits a shorter last sub-batch, or carries leftovers to the next batch, that needs to be stated. This affects both reproducibility and fairness of the reported comparisons.

comments.title: Use of an old PyTorch version is not a valid control for sampler effects
comments.quote: The reason why we chose old version PyTorch is, the performance of the old version may not be so powelful, however, the experimental effect may be better, since the contrast of the results may be larger. And since the old version may not have too many functions, it can focus on the factor of “repeat input”, without the interferences from other irrelevant factors.
comments.explanation: It is acceptable to report and fix a specific software version for reproducibility, and using the same version across all compared methods does control some implementation variation. The stated rationale, however, is questionable. Choosing an older framework because it may produce larger contrasts is not a standard experimental control, and the number of available PyTorch functions does not by itself reduce interference unless the unused functions affect execution. If anything, older kernels and defaults can introduce their own confounders. The comparison can still be meaningful if all methods use the same environment, but this explanation does not justify the choice scientifically.

comments.title: Sampler comparison does not appear to control for effective sample exposures or optimizer updates
comments.quote: The difference of the models is only the sampler, for comparing the results only caused by the sampler.
comments.explanation: I understand the authors' intent: the architecture, learning rate, epoch count, and other hyperparameters are meant to be held fixed so that the stepped sampler is isolated. However, the stepped sampler is not merely a different ordering of the same examples. From the earlier definition and Figure 2, a batch of size \(L\) is divided into overlapping sub-batches of size \(m\) with stride \(n\). For example, the reported setting \(L=25, m=20, n=2\) would create multiple overlapping sub-batches from one original batch, so many examples are presented more than once within an epoch and the number of forward/backward passes or gradient updates may differ from the traditional sampler unless explicitly controlled. Thus the observed faster loss decrease could be due to additional sample exposures, changed effective batch size, or changed number of optimizer steps, not only to the proposed repetition principle. The text does not specify whether epochs are normalized by original dataset passes, by sub-batch updates, by number of unique examples, or by wall-clock/compute budget. This affects the central experimental comparison.

comments.title: Adam optimizer setting is underspecified or inconsistent with standard Adam parameters
comments.quote: We used Adam as the optimization algorithm. ... The learning rate was set to 0.0001. The momentum was set to 0.01.
comments.explanation: The optimizer choice and learning rate are clear, but the reported 'momentum' parameter raises a reproducibility concern. Standard Adam in PyTorch is parameterized by \(\beta_1\), \(\beta_2\), \(\epsilon\), weight decay, etc.; it does not have a single SGD-style momentum argument. The authors may mean \(\beta_1=0.01\), or they may be describing a different optimizer configuration informally, but this is not resolvable from the passage. Since optimizer hyperparameters can strongly affect convergence speed and loss stability—the main outcomes being compared—the exact Adam settings need to be stated in PyTorch terms.

comments.title: Train/test split procedure for UCF101 is underspecified and may affect comparability
comments.quote: UCF101 was split into training set and test set by a ratio of 3:1.
comments.explanation: A 3:1 split is understandable as a simple experimental setup, and the authors are not necessarily claiming direct state-of-the-art comparability here. Still, UCF101 has commonly used official train/test splits, and action-recognition results can vary depending on which videos, subjects, and classes are placed in each split. The passage does not state whether the split is random, stratified by class, based on official split files, fixed by a random seed, or repeated over multiple splits. Because the paper compares relatively close accuracy values across sampler variants, the exact split protocol is important for reproducibility and for assessing whether differences are due to the sampler or to split variance.

comments.title: Data transformation/augmentation is too vague to reproduce
comments.quote: The data transformation was applied to enhance the network. The input frames were transformed into 256 _×_ 342 pixels.
comments.explanation: The resize target is stated, which helps, but 'data transformation' is too broad for reproducibility. In video/action recognition, choices such as frame sampling rate, number of frames per clip, random crop versus center crop, horizontal flip, normalization statistics, color jitter, and whether transformations are applied consistently across frames can materially change accuracy and temporal information. Since the paper attributes performance differences to the sampler, the preprocessing and augmentation pipeline needs to be specified sufficiently to exclude these as hidden confounders.

comments.title: Claims about convergence and stability appear based on single curves without variance or statistical testing
comments.quote: The training loss in Figure 4 (a) has many jitters, even when the epoch is more than 110, while the training loss in the other subfigures are much smoother, and can converge earlier than the traditional batch sampler model (Figure 4 (a)).
comments.explanation: It is reasonable to inspect loss curves as preliminary evidence, and the authors consistently compare models under nominally similar hyperparameters. However, training an LSTM/CNN on UCF101 from scratch is stochastic: initialization, data order, augmentation, GPU nondeterminism, and train/test split can all affect loss jitter and test accuracy. The passage does not report multiple seeds, confidence intervals, standard deviations, or a formal criterion for 'converge earlier' or 'more stable'. Without these, visual differences in a small number of curves may not support the general conclusion that the stepped sampler reliably improves convergence stability.

comments.title: The 'normal distribution' conclusion about stride is not justified by the reported evidence
comments.quote: If the batch size and the step size are fixed, the detection effect seems to be a normal distribution of the step stride.
comments.explanation: The authors are trying to describe the empirical pattern that an intermediate stride works better than very small or very large strides. That qualitative observation may be plausible. But saying the detection effect 'seems to be a normal distribution' is a stronger statistical/mathematical claim: it suggests a specific parametric shape in stride, whereas the passage only describes a small number of stride settings in one experimental condition. A safer statement would be that performance appears unimodal over the tested stride values. As written, the claim over-interprets the limited sweep.

comments.title: Broad-spectrum machine learning claim is overgeneralized from a narrow experiment
comments.quote: The training loss of Figure 5 (c) and Figure 6 (c) converge faster than Figure 5 (a) and Figure 6 (a) , which denotes that, our method may have a broad-spectrum effect on machine learning.
comments.explanation: The authors appropriately use 'may', so this is framed somewhat cautiously. Still, the evidence described here is limited to one CNN-LSTM architecture, one dataset, and a small set of batch/stride settings. Faster convergence in these plots does not establish a broad-spectrum effect across machine learning, or even across temporal models and video datasets. The claim would be better limited to the tested CNN-LSTM/UCF101 setup unless additional experiments on other tasks, architectures, datasets, and seeds are provided.

comments.title: Use of shuffle during testing is ambiguous and the justification from ShuffleNet is not applicable
comments.quote:
Our test uses the shuffle operation. ShuffleNet (Zhang et al., 2018) proves that the shuffle operation can improve the image detection mAP. We analyse that, the reason should be that the shuffle operation can reduce the correlation. According to our definition in Appendix A, the correlation

is the temporal information. Therefore, we consider that the shuffle operation can reduce the temporal information.
comments.explanation: I can see the authors are trying to avoid test-order artifacts and to make evaluation less dependent on temporal continuity outside the model input. However, the passage is ambiguous about what is shuffled: the order of videos/clips in the test loader, the order of frames within each video, or something else. These have very different implications. Shuffling test samples is usually harmless if hidden states are reset per sample, but shuffling frames inside a video would directly destroy the temporal structure that an LSTM action-recognition model is designed to use. The appeal to ShuffleNet also does not resolve this: ShuffleNet's 'shuffle' is channel shuffle inside a CNN architecture, not dataset or frame-order shuffling, and does not imply that reducing temporal correlation improves video recognition evaluation. Because the method centers on temporal information, this ambiguity could materially affect the interpretation of the reported test accuracy.

comments.title: Claim that shuffling test data improves evaluation is not well supported
comments.quote: Since the test data is shuffled, there should be less temporal information among the data, the test results may reflect the detection effect better.
comments.explanation: The authors may intend that randomizing sample order prevents accidental temporal carryover between consecutive test items. If so, that is a standard practice, provided model state is reset appropriately. But the stated rationale is problematic: for an action-recognition task, temporal information is part of the signal to be evaluated, not necessarily a confound to be removed. If the shuffle is only between independent videos, it should not reduce within-video temporal information; if it is within videos, it changes the task. The passage therefore leaves a careful reader unsure why reducing temporal information at test time would make the result 'better' or more faithful to the model's intended use.

comments.title: Training-time explanation is unsupported and may conflict with the extra sub-batch computation
comments.quote: However, since the repeated data are the same, the training time of a sub-batch is much shorter than an ordinary batch. Therefore, for the total training time, the stepped sampler and the traditional sampler are almost the same.
comments.explanation: It is useful that the authors discuss computational cost and give an approximate wall-clock comparison. However, the explanation that repeated data make a sub-batch much faster is not generally true in standard PyTorch training: unless there is a special caching mechanism or skipped computation, the same tensors still require forward and backward passes. A sub-batch may be faster because it is smaller than the original batch, but multiple overlapping sub-batches can increase the total number of computations and optimizer steps. The reported 60-hour example helps, but the mechanism and measurement protocol are not specified enough to substantiate the general statement that total training time is almost the same.

comments.title: Stepped-sampler pseudocode is ambiguous about whether it yields multiple sub-batches or terminates after the first one
comments.quote:
**if** _len(step_ ~~_b_~~ _atch[]) == m_ **then** // when the size of step ~~b~~ atch reaches m return step batch[]; // output the sub-batch Reset step ~~b~~ atch[] to empty set;

_idx_ = _idx − m_ + _n_ ; // move the coordinate to the next sub-batch by stride n
comments.explanation: I understand the intended operation: once a sub-batch of size m is formed, it should be emitted, then the internal buffer should be reset and the starting position advanced by stride n so that overlapping sub-batches are produced. That intent is consistent with the surrounding prose and figures. However, the pseudocode says `return step batch[]`; in standard algorithm notation and in Python, `return` exits the procedure, so the subsequent reset and index update would not execute. If the authors mean `yield` or “output and continue,” that should be stated explicitly. This is not just a wording issue, because the number of optimization steps per original batch is central to the method and to the comparison with the BatchSampler baseline.

comments.title: Index update inside a for-loop makes the sampler procedure non-reproducible as written
comments.quote:
**for** _idx_ = 1 _,_ 2 _, · · · , L_ **do** // traverse the elements in a batch of the batch sampler

output the idx-th item batch[idx] into step ~~b~~ atch[]; _idx_ + = 1;
comments.explanation: The high-level idea of sliding a window of length m by stride n is clear, and the later assignment `_idx_ = _idx − m_ + _n_` is presumably meant to implement that. But the algorithm is written as a `for idx = 1,2,...,L` loop while also manually changing `idx` inside the loop. In many programming languages and in ordinary pseudocode conventions, the loop variable is automatically advanced by the loop construct, so `_idx_ += 1` either has no effect on the next iteration or causes off-by-one/skipping behavior depending on implementation. A careful reader cannot tell whether the intended implementation is a while-loop over start positions, a generator over windows, or a literal traversal of all elements. Since the paper’s empirical claims depend on exactly which samples are repeated and how often, this ambiguity materially affects reproducibility.

comments.title: Boundary behavior is underspecified when the stride does not evenly tile the original batch
comments.quote: **Input** : Dataset, batch size L, step size m, step stride n, and _L > m ≥ n_
comments.explanation: The stated constraints ensure that the sub-batch length and stride are well ordered, but they do not specify what happens when `L - m` is not divisible by `n`. This matters for reported settings such as batch size 25, step size 20, stride 2 or 3: a sliding-window interpretation gives starts such as 1, 3, 5, ... and the final valid window may leave tail elements of the original batch unused, or alternatively one might include a shorter final sub-batch, pad, wrap around, or force the last window to end at L. The context says PyTorch rounds down the iteration number when non-integer, which suggests dropping some tail positions, but the algorithm comment also says it traverses all data. If some positions are systematically dropped within each batch under the sequential sampler, then the method changes not only repetition but also sample exposure. The paper should make the boundary rule explicit and report whether all training examples are seen equally often.

comments.title: The comparison called “same batch size” appears to confound sampler type with effective mini-batch size and number of updates
comments.quote: The above six models are all with batch size 25. Subfigure (a) is a traditional LSTM model, which uses the batch sampler in PyTorch. Subfigure (b) is with the proposed stepped sampler, whose batch size is 25, step size is 20, step stride is 1. Subfigure (c), (d), (e), (f) are with the stepped sampler, whose batch size is 25, step size is 20, step stride is 2, 3, 4, 5, respectively.
comments.explanation: I see why the authors describe all models as having batch size 25: L is the original batch emitted by the PyTorch BatchSampler. But the actual units returned to the model by the stepped sampler appear to be sub-batches of size m, e.g. 20 rather than 25 in Figure 4, and there may be multiple sub-batches/gradient updates per original batch. Thus the baseline and stepped models may differ simultaneously in sampler structure, effective optimizer mini-batch size, number of parameter updates per epoch, and number of repeated exposures to examples. Faster loss decrease per epoch could therefore be due to more gradient steps or different batch-size noise rather than an Ebbinghaus-like memory effect. To isolate the sampler effect, the paper would need to control or at least report comparisons at matched numbers of optimizer updates, matched total sample presentations, and matched effective mini-batch sizes.

comments.title: Claim of significantly higher accuracy is not supported by statistical evidence
comments.quote: When the batch size is 15, the test accuracy of the stepped sampler LSTM is significantly higher than that of the traditional sampler with the same batch size.
comments.explanation: Table 2 does show that the stride-5 stepped model is higher than the BatchSampler model at the listed later epochs, e.g. 0.637 versus 0.616 at epoch 150 and 0.639 versus 0.622 at epoch 120. So the direction of the reported result is not fabricated. The concern is the word “significantly”: the passage gives no repeated runs, standard deviations, confidence intervals, statistical test, or discussion of run-to-run variability. On UCF101 with training from scratch, initialization, data split, frame sampling, and stochastic optimization can all affect accuracy by nontrivial amounts. Without uncertainty estimates, the result can be described as “higher in this run/setting,” but not as statistically significant.

comments.title: Convergence and stability claims are qualitative and not defined quantitatively
comments.quote: The experimental results show that, compared with the traditional sampler, the training loss of the stepped sampler converges faster, and is more stable after the convergence, i.e., there is no large jitter after convergence. The test accuracy of the model with the stepped sampler also reaches a high point faster and is more stable either.
comments.explanation: The figures and tables plausibly motivate the authors’ observation that some stepped-sampler curves look smoother and reach good accuracy earlier. However, the passage does not define a convergence criterion, a stability metric, or a jitter measure, and the tables show mixed behavior across settings: for example, in Table 1 several stepped variants have substantially lower accuracy than the BatchSampler baseline at many epochs. A visual claim about smoother curves is acceptable as exploratory commentary, but the conclusion states it as a general experimental result. To support this, the paper should quantify convergence epoch, variance of loss after convergence, best/last accuracy, and preferably average these over multiple seeds.

comments.title: Human-like learning interpretation overreaches beyond what the sampler experiment establishes
comments.quote: The experiments seem to verify the similarity between machine learning and human learning. We assume that artificial neural networks have human-like characteristics.
comments.explanation: The Ebbinghaus analogy is a reasonable source of inspiration for a repeated-input sampler, and the authors are careful here to use “seem” and “assume.” Still, the experiment only compares different data-feeding schedules for a CNN-LSTM. Even if the stepped sampler improves convergence or accuracy under some settings, that does not verify that the mechanism is analogous to human memory, timely review, or human-like forgetting. The observed effects could arise from ordinary machine-learning factors such as oversampling, altered gradient noise, curriculum/order effects, or more optimizer steps per epoch. This should be framed as a motivating analogy or hypothesis, not as something verified by the reported evidence.

comments.title: Experimental sampler results do not establish a close biological–artificial neural-network relationship
comments.quote: The results show that, there may be a close relationship between biological neural network and artificial neural network, whatever in structure and even in principle.
comments.explanation: I can see the authors' intent: the paper is motivated by an analogy between Ebbinghaus-style repetition in human learning and repeated/overlapping input to an LSTM, and the reported experiments do suggest that some stepped-sampler settings can change convergence behavior. However, checking the surrounding context, the experiments compare only CNN-LSTM training with and without a particular data sampler on UCF101; they do not measure biological neural networks, cognitive learning, or structural/principled equivalence between biological and artificial systems. The LSTM architecture is also explicitly unchanged, so the evidence is about an optimization/data-ordering intervention, not about structural similarity. Even with the cautious word “may,” the statement substantially over-interprets the experimental evidence.

comments.title: Hyperparameter-dependent accuracy is used to infer human individual-difference effects without support
comments.quote: How to improve human learning is different for each individual, and the test accuracy of our experiment may illustrate this point, and we believe that is why not all the stepped sampler LSTM’s test accuracy is higher than the traditional sampler.
comments.explanation: The authors are trying to interpret an important empirical nuance correctly: not every stepped-sampler configuration improves test accuracy, and the paper elsewhere notes that stride/step settings appear problem-specific. That part is reasonable. What remains problematic is the leap from hyperparameter sensitivity in one model/dataset setting to an analogy with differences between individual human learners. The observed variation could be explained by ordinary machine-learning factors—effective batch size, duplicated samples, altered gradient noise, optimization dynamics, stochastic initialization, or overfitting—without invoking human individual differences. The context does not provide experiments across different architectures, datasets, or cognitive analogues that would justify this explanation.

comments.title: Claims that attention and transfer learning were inspired by human learning methods are under-justified
comments.quote: The attention mechanism (Vaswani et al., 2017) may be also inspired by human learning methods. Transfer learning (Bozinovski & Fulgosi, 1976) is using old knowledge to learn new knowledge, which may be inspired by human learning methods either.
comments.explanation: I understand why the authors include these examples: both attention and transfer learning are often described using cognitively intuitive language, and transfer learning in particular has an obvious analogy to applying prior knowledge. The wording is softened with “may,” so this is not presented as a formal claim. Still, the cited Transformer paper motivates attention mainly as a neural sequence-modeling mechanism and computational alternative to recurrence/convolution, not specifically as a human learning method; similarly, transfer learning is a technical machine-learning paradigm whose historical motivation is broader than human learning. Without citations from cognitive science or from the cited works supporting this inspiration claim, these examples read as speculative rather than evidential support for the paper’s broader biological analogy.

comments.title: “Faster learning effect” is broader than what the reported metrics directly establish
comments.quote: Compared with the traditional sampler, the stepped sampler LSTM has a faster learning effect, and has a higher test accuracy under certain parameters.
comments.explanation: The second half of the sentence is appropriately qualified: the full context does show that some stepped-sampler settings, especially in the batch-size-15 case, reportedly achieve higher test accuracy, while others do not. My concern is with “faster learning effect.” Earlier sections mainly support this through smoother and faster-decreasing training-loss curves over epochs. But because the stepped sampler changes the number and composition of sub-batches/updates seen within an epoch, an epoch-to-epoch comparison may not correspond to the same number of optimizer steps, same number of effective sample presentations, or same amount of computation. The paper later states training time is similar under one condition, but the conclusion here does not distinguish faster loss reduction per epoch, per update, or per wall-clock time. A careful reader could therefore overread this as a general computational speedup or learning-rate improvement.
